nativeRange.habitat: None — Echinodorus 'Rose' is a hybrid cultivar with no wild population. The parent genus Echinodorus is native to wetlands of the tropical Americas (marshes, riverbanks, seasonally flooded lowlands).

decorPreferences: moderate to bright lighting for best coloration

imageCandidates.notes: Product photo of Rose Sword (Echinodorus sp. Rose) from Modern Aquarium's listing. Shows the characteristic lance-shaped leaves with pink-rose new growth. Retail product image; copyright holder is Modern Aquarium.

summary: A hybrid Echinodorus cultivar prized for the pink-to-rose tinted new leaves that mature to bright or dark green. Classic background sword plant for medium-to-large planted tanks; same easy care as other Echinodorus, with brighter light deepening the coloration.
careNotes: Echinodorus 'Rose' is a HYBRID cultivar of unknown specific parentage — sold across the trade as Echinodorus sp. 'Rose' or 'Rose Sword' with no recognised wild population. The parent genus Echinodorus is native to wetlands of the tropical Americas (Alismataceae). Adult size is reported as roughly 10–20 in (25–50 cm), consistent with the wider Amazon-sword complex; under strong light and rich substrate it grows into a large background specimen, while in lower light it stays more compact. Lighting is moderate by default — Modern Aquarium and Buce Plant both note that brighter light enhances the signature pink/rose coloration of new growth and produces a tighter habit, while low light is tolerated but yields paler leaves and a laxer form. CO2 is not required (both sources agree) but injection improves leaf size, density, and colour. This is a HEAVY ROOT FEEDER like every other Echinodorus: plant in a deep nutrient-rich substrate or aquarium soil, supplement with root tabs near the root zone (not directly under the crown), and keep the crown just above the substrate surface. Temperature tolerance follows the general Echinodorus range — 20–28 °C is comfortable, with the wider tolerance reported as 68–82 °F (20–28 °C) by Modern Aquarium. Buce Plant lists a low pH range of 5.0–5.8 for ideal display; in practice the plant tolerates pH up to ~7.5 and gH 4–18, matching the rest of the Echinodorus complex. Place as a background plant in larger tanks or as a midground specimen in extra-tall setups. Newly purchased plants may melt back emersed leaves before sending up the longer, more lance-shaped submerged form — this is normal. Avoid drastic parameter swings (especially from soft acidic to hard alkaline) to minimise melt.
breedingNotes: Propagates vegetatively by runners that produce daughter plants; once each plantlet has a small rosette and visible roots it can be cut from the runner and replanted. Like other Echinodorus, mature specimens can also produce adventitious plantlets on long emergent flower stalks and may be divided at the crown. As a hybrid cultivar, seed propagation is not commercially used; runner propagation is the standard method and is easy enough that runners may need trimming to control spread.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium — Rose Sword (Echinodorus sp. Rose)
primary.notes: Manifest-specified primary source. Confirmed temperature 68–82 °F (20–28 °C), moderate light (brighter light enhances coloration and tighter growth), CO2 not required, medium growth rate, background placement, runner propagation, heavy root feeder needing root tabs (not directly on crown), iron/micronutrient supplementation for deeper colour, easy care level. Common name 'Rose Sword'.

additional.name: Buce Plant — Echinodorus Rose
additional.notes: Secondary source. Confirmed family Alismataceae, origin 'Tropical Western Hemisphere', size 10–20 in (25–50 cm), pH 5.0–5.8 (Buce's preferred display range), moderate lighting (higher light enhances coloration), CO2 optional, medium growth rate, mid- or background placement, runner propagation, heavy root feeder requiring nutrient-rich substrate / aquarium soil, easy care level. Notes new foliage emerges pink/bronze and matures to bright or dark green.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Echinodorus
additional.notes: Used for genus-level taxonomy (family Alismataceae, order Alismatales) and confirmation that Echinodorus species/hybrids are American wetland marsh/bog plants that grow submersed, and that named forms in the aquarium trade frequently arise from artificial hybridisation — supporting treatment of 'Rose' as a trade cultivar without a single wild origin.
